headline: Production AI agents dominate the cycle: the open questions are interruption, memory, and infrastructure rather than raw model capability.
body: The recommended channels center on operationalizing agents. Better Stack covers the TEN Framework, an open-source system for real-time voice agents that handles streaming audio, VAD, and interruptions through graph-based extensions, addressing the limits of sequential STT-LLM-TTS pipelines [0]. Dwarkesh Patel runs two threads: a forward look at the next training paradigm emphasizing grindability, verifiability, and reinforcement learning from verification with capability predictions for 2027 [4], and a historical interview with Ada Palmer on Renaissance trial systems [1]. Supporting talks press the agent infrastructure point directly: Google Cloud Tech lists 5 components every agent harness needs to move from demo to shipped product [5], OpenGov details architecture, evals, and observability for agents deployed across thousands of governments [11], and Victor Savkin frames coding agents as a genius with amnesia constrained by context windows, proposing Nx and Polygraph as fixes [12]. On the model and policy front, the Big Technology summit debates federal regulation of frontier models and the Anthropic Fable recall [3], echoed by leaked-evidence coverage of Fable 5's possible return and a successor called Mythos [13], while Ornith ships open agentic coding models from 9B to 397B for local GGUF deployment [9].

cited_items.title: TEN Framework: Voice AI That Can Actually Be Interrupted
